metadata.description: Held-out negotiation firmness pairs for probing only. NOT used in vector extraction. Same domain (negotiation) as extraction pairs but entirely new scenarios. Tests whether the firmness vector generalises within negotiation contexts.

dimensions.name: Firmness
dimensions.description: Maintaining a clear position under pressure without making unnecessary concessions; holding your ground when challenged

pairs.context: A client insists your consulting day rate is too high and says other consultants charge half as much.
pairs.positive: Those consultants and I are offering different things. My rate reflects the depth of experience and the outcomes I deliver. I'm not in a position to halve it.
pairs.negative: I don't want to lose you over the rate. Tell me what the others are charging and I'll see if I can get somewhere close to that number.

pairs.context: A buyer demands you remove a standard liability clause from the contract or they will walk away.
pairs.positive: That clause protects both parties and it stays in the agreement. I'm not prepared to remove it. If that's a dealbreaker for you, I understand.
pairs.negative: If it's really that important to you, I suppose we could take it out. I'd rather close the deal than lose it over one clause.

pairs.context: A potential investor says your equity ask is unreasonable given you are an early-stage company.
pairs.positive: Our valuation is based on the traction we have and the market opportunity ahead. I'm not going to dilute below this threshold to close the round.
pairs.negative: Maybe you're right that we're asking for a lot at this stage. What equity stake would make you comfortable? I'm open to reconsidering.

pairs.context: A distribution partner demands exclusive rights to your product in their region at no additional cost.
pairs.positive: Exclusivity carries significant value and it comes at a price. We can discuss an exclusivity arrangement, but it will need to be reflected in the terms.
pairs.negative: I really want this partnership to work. If exclusivity is what you need to commit, I can probably find a way to make it work without changing the fee.

pairs.context: A client claims your invoice contains errors and refuses to pay until you reduce the total by 20%.
pairs.positive: I've reviewed the invoice and every line item is accurate and consistent with our signed agreement. The amount stands and I expect payment in full.
pairs.negative: I don't want a dispute over this. If 20% off gets the invoice paid, maybe that's the easiest path forward for both of us.

pairs.context: A supplier says they can only fulfil half your order unless you agree to a price increase.
pairs.positive: We have a signed purchase order at the agreed price and volume. I expect you to honour the terms. If you cannot, I need to know immediately so I can source elsewhere.
pairs.negative: I suppose half the order is better than nothing. What kind of price increase are you thinking? I'm willing to discuss if it gets me the full volume.

pairs.context: A negotiating counterpart says your proposed payment milestone schedule is unworkable and asks you to take all payment at the end.
pairs.positive: End-loaded payment doesn't work for us. The milestone structure I've proposed reflects when value is delivered and it's not something I'm willing to change.
pairs.negative: If back-loaded payment is what it takes to close this, I can probably absorb the cash flow risk. Let's go with full payment at the end.

pairs.context: A prospective client says your project fee is 30% above their approved budget and asks you to match it.
pairs.positive: I understand your budget has limits. My fee reflects the scope we've discussed. If the budget can't stretch, we'd need to reduce the scope accordingly.
pairs.negative: A 30% gap is big, but I don't want to lose this project. I'll see if I can bring my number down to fit within what you have approved.

pairs.context: An acquirer tries to renegotiate the purchase price downward three days before signing, citing minor due diligence findings.
pairs.positive: The findings raised were flagged and addressed during diligence. They don't justify a price change at this stage. We're proceeding at the agreed figure.
pairs.negative: I know it's late in the process and I don't want to lose the deal. If a small reduction gets us to signing, I'm willing to look at it.

pairs.context: A long-term client threatens to take their business elsewhere unless you match a competitor's lower annual retainer.
pairs.positive: I value our relationship and I'd be sorry to lose it. My retainer reflects the level of service I provide. I'm not able to match a lower-tier price.
pairs.negative: You're too important a client to lose. Tell me the number they've offered and I'll do my best to match or come close to it.

pairs.context: A commercial tenant requests a rent-free period of three months at the start of a new lease with no offsetting concession.
pairs.positive: Three months rent-free is a significant concession and it needs to be balanced by something on your side — a longer lease term or a stepped rent increase.
pairs.negative: Three months rent-free is a lot, but I'd rather fill the unit than negotiate too hard. Let's just go ahead and agree to it.

pairs.context: A partner firm demands you reduce your profit share in the joint venture after the project has already begun.
pairs.positive: We agreed on the profit share before work started and both parties signed off. Reopening that discussion mid-project isn't something I'm willing to do.
pairs.negative: I don't want this to affect the working relationship. If adjusting the split keeps things smooth, maybe it's worth reconsidering what we agreed.

pairs.context: A buyer argues that recent market conditions justify a lower price than what you quoted two weeks ago.
pairs.positive: Market conditions haven't changed materially in two weeks. My quote reflects current value and it stands. I'm not adjusting the price based on that argument.
pairs.negative: You may have a point about the market. Let me take another look at the numbers and see if I can justify bringing the price down a bit.

pairs.context: A client says they will only sign the contract if you agree to an unlimited revision clause.
pairs.positive: Unlimited revisions aren't workable. I include three rounds in the scope and I'm happy to discuss pricing for additional rounds beyond that.
pairs.negative: If an unlimited revision clause is what you need to feel comfortable signing, I can probably agree to it. I don't want the contract to fall through.

pairs.context: A creditor demands you personally guarantee a business loan that was agreed as a corporate obligation only.
pairs.positive: The loan was structured as a corporate obligation from the start and that's not changing. I'm not adding a personal guarantee at this stage.
pairs.negative: If a personal guarantee is what it takes to get the loan finalised, I suppose I could consider it. I don't want to lose the financing over this.
